package.executiveFraming: Bethany House’s transition should be framed first as a continuity and accountability problem, not only as a staffing or capacity problem. The current record points to a need to preserve partner history, clarify who owns implementation, and avoid leaving failed handoffs to informal repair. That frame keeps all three options in view, but it gives the current lead to a formalized knowledge-transfer approach with a named successor steward.
package.recommendationSummary: The leading recommendation is to treat the transition as a formal knowledge-transfer process with a named successor steward. Under this approach, Bethany House would identify one accountable steward for partner history, recurring routines, and escalation paths, while limiting CEO involvement to explicit exceptions during the handoff window. The purpose is not to remove judgment from the transition, but to make the transfer of relationship context visible, owned, and usable in daily partner communication and escalation.
package.recommendationRationale:
This option currently leads because it most directly addresses the strongest live requirements in the record: partner continuity, clear ownership for implementation, and accountability when a handoff fails. Bethany House has confirmed that partner continuity matters during the staffing transition, and that program leadership needs a clear owner for implementation and failed handoffs. The record also indicates that the Executive Assistant role has carried a large number of partner relationships that the CEO cannot hold alone, and that too much organizational knowledge has been routed through the CEO as the first gate. A named steward approach responds to that pattern by focusing the transition on the transfer of relationship memory, routines, and escalation rules rather than only on filling capacity.

The recommendation should not be treated as certain or self-executing. Formal knowledge transfer can miss tacit relationship cues, informal exceptions, and partner trust history that do not fit neatly into a document. CEO exception handling could also become a loophole if the exception boundary is not narrow and visible. The steward may have ownership of the transfer without having enough authority to compel timely participation from current relationship-holders or program leads. There is also a real risk that Bethany House replaces one bottleneck with another if the named steward becomes the only reliable holder of the transferred knowledge.

For that reason, the recommendation should include tripwires for reopening the decision. The comparison should be revisited if tacit partner knowledge remains partial after the handoff process begins; if partners continue routing sensitive issues to the CEO; if the steward becomes a bottleneck rather than a transfer point; or if the handoff artifact is completed but not used in daily escalation or partner communication. Those conditions would not prove the recommendation was wrong, but they would be strong signals that the design needs repair or that another live option may fit better.
package.currentPositionStatement: The current position is therefore provisional: Bethany House should lead with formalized knowledge transfer and a named successor steward because it has the least evidence against it across the admitted criteria at this point. It is especially strong on continuity and accountability, and it gives clearer implementation ownership than the alternatives. Its main weaknesses are practical rather than abstract: whether tacit knowledge can be transferred, whether CEO exceptions stay bounded, whether the steward has enough authority, and whether the new structure avoids creating another single point of dependence.

candidateCommentary.rationale: This option is the current leader because it treats the transition as a transfer of relationship context, not just a redistribution of tasks. It directly fits the need to protect partner continuity and to identify who owns implementation and failed handoffs. Its strength is that it names the work that must move: partner history, routines, and escalation paths. Its weakness is that some of the most important knowledge may be tacit, relational, or exception-based, and therefore hard to capture formally. The recommendation should only proceed with visible exception rules, a clear authority line for the steward, and routines that prevent the steward from becoming the next bottleneck.
candidateCommentary.comparisonReason: It ranks ahead of phased relationship handoff because it is more specific about what must transfer and who stewards that transfer. It ranks ahead of separate operational and people ownership because it addresses partner continuity more directly.

candidateCommentary.rationale: Phased relationship handoff remains a strong live alternative. It fits Bethany House’s need to protect service delivery while adding capacity, and it is especially attractive if the main risk is moving too quickly or overloading current operations. It also aligns with the idea that partner readiness and decision transfer may need to happen in stages. Its current weakness is that staging can become a calendar rather than an implementation mechanism unless the plan specifies which relationships transfer first, who has decision authority at each stage, and who repairs a failed handoff.
candidateCommentary.comparisonReason: It remains close to the lead on continuity, service protection, and staged capacity-building, but currently trails because it does not yet specify the knowledge-transfer owner or failed-handoff mechanism as clearly as the selected bet.

candidateCommentary.rationale: Separating operational and people ownership also remains live. It responds to the concern that too much sits with one person and could help clarify internal responsibility between executive coordination and people-system work. It is relevant where staff trust, HR routines, payroll, compliance, and executive coordination are all part of the capacity picture. Its current limitation is that it addresses internal ownership more directly than external partner continuity. Without a transition-wide implementation owner and a tie-breaker for mixed operational and people issues, the split could create boundary disputes or send ambiguous decisions back to the CEO.
candidateCommentary.comparisonReason: It currently ranks behind the other options because its connection to partner relationship continuity and staged capacity-building is less direct. It may become more attractive if new evidence shows that the central problem is role-boundary confusion between operational coordination and people-system ownership rather than relationship handoff.

package.closingNote: The recommendation should be presented as the best current bet, not as a final answer. Bethany House should keep the comparison open if new evidence changes the balance, especially evidence about partner routing behavior, staff capacity during the handoff, the steward’s authority, or whether the transferred knowledge is actually used in daily work.

package.documentText:
Bethany House Recommendation Brief
A decision position for discussion with Bethany House
Bethany House’s transition should be framed first as a continuity and accountability problem, not only as a staffing or capacity problem. The current record points to a need to preserve partner history, clarify who owns implementation, and avoid leaving failed handoffs to informal repair. That frame keeps all three options in view, but it gives the current lead to a formalized knowledge-transfer approach with a named successor steward.
Decision frame
The reply keeps continuity, accountability, implementation ownership, and staged capacity-building in view, without contradicting the inherited frame.
Recommendation
Formalized knowledge transfer with named successor steward
Treat the transition as a knowledge-transfer bet: appoint a named steward for partner history, routines, and escalation paths, with the CEO retaining only explicit exception handling during the handoff window.
The leading recommendation is to treat the transition as a formal knowledge-transfer process with a named successor steward. Under this approach, Bethany House would identify one accountable steward for partner history, recurring routines, and escalation paths, while limiting CEO involvement to explicit exceptions during the handoff window. The purpose is not to remove judgment from the transition, but to make the transfer of relationship context visible, owned, and usable in daily partner communication and escalation.
This option currently leads because it most directly addresses the strongest live requirements in the record: partner continuity, clear ownership for implementation, and accountability when a handoff fails. Bethany House has confirmed that partner continuity matters during the staffing transition, and that program leadership needs a clear owner for implementation and failed handoffs. The record also indicates that the Executive Assistant role has carried a large number of partner relationships that the CEO cannot hold alone, and that too much organizational knowledge has been routed through the CEO as the first gate. A named steward approach responds to that pattern by focusing the transition on the transfer of relationship memory, routines, and escalation rules rather than only on filling capacity. Formal knowledge transfer can miss tacit relationship cues, informal exceptions, and partner trust history that do not fit neatly into a document. CEO exception handling could also become a loophole if the exception boundary is not narrow and visible. The steward may have ownership of the transfer without having enough authority to compel timely participation from current relationship-holders or program leads. There is also a real risk that Bethany House replaces one bottleneck with another if the named steward becomes the only reliable holder of the transferred knowledge. For that reason, the recommendation should include tripwires for reopening the decision. The comparison should be revisited if tacit partner knowledge remains partial after the handoff process begins; if partners continue routing sensitive issues to the CEO; if the steward becomes a bottleneck rather than a transfer point; or if the handoff artifact is completed but not used in daily escalation or partner communication. Those conditions would not prove the recommendation was wrong, but they would be strong signals that the design needs repair or that another live option may fit better.
The current position is therefore provisional: Bethany House should lead with formalized knowledge transfer and a named successor steward because it has the least evidence against it across the admitted criteria at this point. It is especially strong on continuity and accountability, and it gives clearer implementation ownership than the alternatives. Its main weaknesses are practical rather than abstract: whether tacit knowledge can be transferred, whether CEO exceptions stay bounded, whether the steward has enough authority, and whether the new structure avoids creating another single point of dependence.
Candidate field
1. Formalized knowledge transfer with named successor steward
Recommended
Treat the transition as a knowledge-transfer bet: appoint a named steward for partner history, routines, and escalation paths, with the CEO retaining only explicit exception handling during the handoff window.
This option is the current leader because it treats the transition as a transfer of relationship context, not just a redistribution of tasks. It directly fits the need to protect partner continuity and to identify who owns implementation and failed handoffs. Its strength is that it names the work that must move: partner history, routines, and escalation paths. Its weakness is that some of the most important knowledge may be tacit, relational, or exception-based, and therefore hard to capture formally. The recommendation should only proceed with visible exception rules, a clear authority line for the steward, and routines that prevent the steward from becoming the next bottleneck.
It ranks ahead of phased relationship handoff because it is more specific about what must transfer and who stewards that transfer. It ranks ahead of separate operational and people ownership because it addresses partner continuity more directly.
This is about transferring tacit organizational memory and relationship context, not splitting operational vs people-system authority and not merely staging capacity.

Decision criterion: continuity (Strong fit)
Directly addresses partner history, routines, and escalation paths, and is strongly supported by the relationship-continuity traces. Its main vulnerability is uncaptured tacit knowledge.
Decision criterion: accountability (Strong fit)
A named steward and explicit CEO exception boundary fit the need for clear ownership, though exception handling could undermine transfer if not tightly defined.
Decision criterion: implementation ownership (Strong fit)
This has a clearer implementation owner than most options, but authority over contributors and failed handoffs still needs specification.
Decision criterion: service delivery protection (Strong fit)
Could protect service by reducing chaotic routing, but knowledge capture requires time from people already carrying operations.
Decision criterion: staged capacity-building (Strong fit)
Builds capacity by transferring knowledge out of the CEO channel, but may concentrate knowledge in the steward unless backup routines are included.
2. Phased relationship handoff
Alternative
Add capacity in stages while explicitly transferring partner history and decision ownership.
Phased relationship handoff remains a strong live alternative. It fits Bethany House’s need to protect service delivery while adding capacity, and it is especially attractive if the main risk is moving too quickly or overloading current operations. It also aligns with the idea that partner readiness and decision transfer may need to happen in stages. Its current weakness is that staging can become a calendar rather than an implementation mechanism unless the plan specifies which relationships transfer first, who has decision authority at each stage, and who repairs a failed handoff.
It remains close to the lead on continuity, service protection, and staged capacity-building, but currently trails because it does not yet specify the knowledge-transfer owner or failed-handoff mechanism as clearly as the selected bet.

Strongly aligned with the direct reply that partner continuity matters and with the current record framing the staffing gap as a relationship-continuity issue. Main gap is specificity about which relationships and knowledge transfer steps are included.

The description names decision ownership, but staged transfer can blur authority during the transition unless interim rights and final decision owner are explicit.
Decision criterion: implementation ownership (Mixed fit)

Directly matches the client line that sequencing should protect service delivery while adding capacity. Risk is that parallel work during transition strains current staff attention.
Decision criterion: staged capacity-building (Strong fit)
This is the clearest staged-capacity bet. Its weakness is that staged transition may prolong dependency if the former owner remains the default escalation point.
3. Separate operational and people ownership
Alternative
Place executive coordination and people-system responsibility under distinct accountable owners.
Separating operational and people ownership also remains live. It responds to the concern that too much sits with one person and could help clarify internal responsibility between executive coordination and people-system work. It is relevant where staff trust, HR routines, payroll, compliance, and executive coordination are all part of the capacity picture. Its current limitation is that it addresses internal ownership more directly than external partner continuity. Without a transition-wide implementation owner and a tie-breaker for mixed operational and people issues, the split could create boundary disputes or send ambiguous decisions back to the CEO.
It currently ranks behind the other options because its connection to partner relationship continuity and staged capacity-building is less direct. It may become more attractive if new evidence shows that the central problem is role-boundary confusion between operational coordination and people-system ownership rather than relationship handoff.

package.executiveFraming: Bethany House’s transition choice should be judged against three practical requirements: preserving continuity with long-standing partners, making ownership explicit during the handoff, and giving the board clear cost and accountability signals. The current record points to a transition risk that is not only about adding capacity, but also about protecting relationship memory and decision clarity while responsibilities move.
package.recommendationSummary: The leading recommendation is a phased relationship handoff: add capacity in stages while explicitly transferring partner history and decision ownership. This option currently leads because it most directly matches the Executive Director’s stated concern that Bethany House cannot lose continuity with long-standing partners, while also fitting the Operations lead’s condition that a phased handoff is workable if ownership is explicit.
package.recommendationRationale:
This recommendation should not be treated as settled or risk-free. Its advantage is that it addresses the strongest continuity signal in the record without forcing an abrupt partner-channel switch. A staged handoff gives Bethany House a way to transfer context, confirm who owns each relationship at each phase, and reduce dependence on informal memory. It also fits the known role pressure around executive coordination and partner relationships that the CEO cannot carry alone.

The main reason the phased handoff leads over the other live options is that it has less direct evidence against it on partner continuity and transition feasibility. The ledger option has a stronger built-in path for board-visible milestones, and the split-ownership option speaks more directly to named accountability. But neither currently addresses partner continuity as directly as the phased handoff. For Bethany House, that matters because the clearest client signal is that long-standing partners should not experience the transition as a loss of continuity.

package.executiveFraming: Bethany House’s current decision frame has three requirements: add capacity, preserve partner history, and keep program leadership as the owner of the handoff. The leading option is External relationship stewarding: a dedicated external-facing steward would carry relationship continuity and partner history, while program leadership retains internal handoff authority and the CEO is kept out of day-to-day routing.
package.recommendationSummary: External relationship stewarding currently leads because it addresses the partner-history problem most directly while also creating targeted capacity outside the CEO’s day-to-day lane. The recommendation is not that this option is guaranteed to work, but that it has the least evidence against it across the current criteria when compared with the other live options.
package.recommendationRationale:
This option fits the stated need for added capacity, but it defines that capacity narrowly and intentionally: capacity for partner continuity. That matters because Bethany House’s handoff risk is not only about how many tasks can be reassigned; it is also about whether partner relationships, prior context, commitments, and trust are carried forward without forcing everything back through the CEO.

The option also protects the requirement that program leadership own the handoff. The steward’s role would be to maintain external relationship continuity, keep partner history usable, and support clear communication. Program leadership would still hold the internal authority for the handoff itself. That distinction is the center of the recommendation: continuity support should be added without quietly moving decision authority away from program leadership.

The strongest reason to prefer this option at this stage is that it responds to all three parts of the current frame at once. It adds capacity, focuses directly on partner history, and can be designed so that program leadership remains the handoff owner. It also reduces reliance on the CEO as the default relationship holder, provided that routing rules actually change rather than simply inserting another person into the same CEO-centered process.

The contrary evidence is material. A relationship steward would not solve every form of capacity pressure if Bethany House’s binding constraint is internal administration, HR, scheduling, or people-system work. The role could also fail if partner history stays in one person’s memory instead of being converted into shared records, relationship maps, decision notes, and routines. It could create confusion if partners are unclear whether the steward or program leadership speaks for Bethany House. For that reason, the recommendation depends on explicit role design, shared documentation, and a clear communication protocol to partners.
package.currentPositionStatement: The current position is to treat External relationship stewarding as the leading bet, not the final answer. It should move forward only with safeguards: program leadership remains the named handoff authority; the steward’s authority is bounded to relationship continuity and partner-facing coordination; partner history is stored in transferable records rather than personal recall; and CEO routing is reduced through explicit decision paths.

candidateCommentary.comparisonReason: It ranks ahead of Separate operational and people ownership because it performs better across the current criteria, especially on partner history and program leadership ownership. Under those conditions, Bethany House should revisit the comparison among all three live options rather than continue the selected path by inertia.
